The Sphere Packing Problem

Definition 1.

Fix a dimension r∈ℕ. Consider the vector space ℝr, equipped with Euclidean distance and Lebesgue measure Vol⁢(⋅). For x∈ℝr and ε>0, let Br⁢(x,ε) denote the open ball in ℝr, with center x and radius ε.

  1. 1.

    A sphere packing of ℝr is a set C of points in ℝr that have pairwise distance at least two.

  2. 2.

    The upper density of a sphere packing C is

    δ¯r⁢(C):=lim supR→∞Vol⁢(⋃x∈CB⁢(x,1)∩B⁢(0,R))Vol⁢(B⁢(0,R)).

    The sphere-packing constant is Δr:=supCδ¯r⁢(C) where the supremum ranges over all sphere packings C.

  3. 3.

    A lattice packing of ℝr is a sphere packing C which is also a lattice in ℝr, i.e., C={∑i=1rai⁢vi:a1,…,ar∈ℤ} for some basis {v1,…,vr} of ℝr. The lattice-packing constant is ΔrL:=supCδ¯r⁢(C) where the supremum ranges over all lattice packings of ℝr.

The sphere packing problem is to determine Δr. It is not even known whether densest sphere packings always need to be attained by lattices:

Open Question 1.1.

Given r≥1, is it true that Δr=ΔrL?

In the literature, the equation Δr=ΔrL is only shown for r∈{1,2,3,8,24}. The case r=3 is known as the Kepler conjecture, proven by Hales [2]. The cases r=8 and r=24 were settled by Viazovska [3] and by Cohn, Kumar, Miller, Radchenko and Viazovska [1], respectively.

References

  • [1] H. Cohn, A. Kumar, S. D. Miller, D. Radchenko, and M. Viazovska (2017) The sphere packing problem in dimension 24. Annals of Mathematics 185 (3), pp. 1017–1033. doi: 10.4007/annals.2017.185.3.8.
  • [2] T. C. Hales (2005) A proof of the Kepler conjecture. Annals of Mathematics 162 (3), pp. 1065–1185. doi: 10.4007/annals.2005.162.1065.
  • [3] M. S. Viazovska (2017) The sphere packing problem in dimension 8. Annals of Mathematics 185 (3), pp. 991–1015. doi: 10.4007/annals.2017.185.3.7.
